schools.summary: The judgments God set before Israel: 'now these are the judgments which thou shalt set before them' — God giving Moses the 'judgments' (the civil and judicial laws, the ordinances for the ordering of Israel's common life), to be set before the people (the judicial law, following the moral law, the Ten Commandments, ch. 20, the application of the law, the principles of the Decalogue worked out in particular cases, the ordering of the commonwealth); the 'judgments' (the mishpatim, the judicial statutes, the case-laws) — the application of the moral law to the life of the nation (the equity of God's law, the principles of justice, mercy, and right); 'which thou shalt set before them' (Moses to set the laws before the people, to teach and enjoin them, the providence, the judicial law given for Israel's polity, the principles abiding, the particular forms passing, the equity of the law a perpetual guide, even where the letter was for Israel) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: Now these are the judgments which thou shalt set before them
the judgments God set before Israel
the judicial law applying the moral law (the Ten Commandments, ch. 20) to life
the equity of God's law, the principles abiding

schools.summary: The law of the Hebrew servant's release in the seventh year: 'if thou buy an Hebrew servant, six years he shall serve: and in the seventh he shall go out free for nothing' — a Hebrew bondservant (sold for debt or poverty) to serve six years and go free in the seventh, without payment (the limited servitude, not perpetual slavery, but a term of six years, the mercy of the law, the bondage limited, the release assured, Deuteronomy 15:12-15, the Sabbatical principle); the six years' service (the limited term) — the bondage bounded (the humane limit); 'in the seventh he shall go out free for nothing' (the release in the seventh year, free, without paying for his freedom, the mercy, the bondservant set free, Deuteronomy 15:12-18, sent away furnished, the type, the year of release, the liberty of the gospel, the jubilee, Leviticus 25:10; Luke 4:18, the redeemed set free, the providence, the law's mercy to the poor and bound) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: If thou buy an Hebrew servant, six years he shall serve: and in the seventh he shall go out free for nothing
the law of the Hebrew servant's release in the seventh year
the bondage limited, the release assured (Deuteronomy 15:12-15)
the release a type of the liberty of the gospel and the jubilee (Leviticus 25:10; Luke 4:18)

schools.summary: The servant going out as he came in: 'if he came in by himself, he shall go out by himself: if he were married, then his wife shall go out with him' — if the servant came in single, he went out single, if married (his wife coming in with him), his wife went out with him (the equity, the man's condition at entrance and release the same, the protection of the family, the wife who came with him going free with him); the single servant (going out as he came) — the equity (the just rule); 'if he were married, then his wife shall go out with him' (the married servant's wife, who came in with him, going free with him, the protection of the family bond, the providence, the equity of the law, the family preserved) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: If he came in by himself, he shall go out by himself: if he were married, then his wife shall go out with him
the servant going out as he came in
the equity of the law, the man's condition the same at entrance and release
the family preserved, the wife going free with him

schools.summary: The wife given by the master remaining with the master: 'if his master have given him a wife, and she have born him sons or daughters; the wife and her children shall be her master's, and he shall go out by himself' — if the master gave the servant a wife (a bondwoman, the master's property), she and her children remaining the master's when the servant went free (the servant going out alone, the rule, the bondwoman and her children the master's, not freed with the husband, the hard case, that would tempt the servant to stay, vv.5-6); the bondwoman and children remaining the master's (her bondage not ended by the husband's release) — the rule of property (the hard provision); 'he shall go out by himself' (the servant going free alone, leaving wife and children, or choosing to stay, vv.5-6, the providence, the hard case that set before the servant the choice — freedom alone, or love and perpetual service, vv.5-6) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: If his master have given him a wife, and she have born him sons or daughters; the wife and her children shall be her master's, and he shall go out by himself
the wife given by the master remaining with the master
the bondwoman and children the master's property
the hard case setting before the servant the choice (vv.5-6)

schools.summary: The servant choosing to stay out of love: 'and if the servant shall plainly say, I love my master, my wife, and my children; I will not go out free' — the servant, loving his master, wife, and children, plainly declaring he would not go out free, choosing to remain in service (the voluntary servitude, the love-bound choice to stay, the declaration, the plain, deliberate confession of love and willing bondage); the servant's love (for master, wife, children) — the ground of the choice (the willing service born of love); 'I will not go out free' (the servant refusing freedom, choosing perpetual service out of love, the voluntary bondage, the love that prefers service to freedom, the providence, the choice that led to the bored ear, the perpetual servant, v.6, a type, the witnesses note, of the love that binds to willing service, Christ's voluntary servitude, Psalm 40:6-8, and the believer's glad bondservice to Christ, Romans 6:18-22) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And if the servant shall plainly say, I love my master, my wife, and my children; I will not go out free
the servant choosing to stay out of love
the voluntary servitude, the love that prefers service to freedom
a type of willing bondservice (Psalm 40:6-8; Romans 6:18-22)

schools.summary: The servant's bored ear and perpetual service: 'then his master shall bring him unto the judges; he shall also bring him to the door, or unto the door post; and his master shall bore his ear through with an aul; and he shall serve him for ever' — the servant who chose to stay brought before the judges (God, the magistrates, to confirm the voluntary act), then to the door, and his ear bored through with an awl (a mark of perpetual servitude, the ear fastened to the door, the solemn rite, the public, witnessed confirmation of the willing bondage, the mark in the ear, the organ of hearing and obedience, the servant bound to hear and obey his master for ever); the bored ear — the ear (the organ of obedience) bored, marking perpetual, willing service (the witnesses noting the lesson and the type — the love-bound servant, who would not go free, bound to his master for ever by the bored ear, many tracing the type to Christ, the willing Servant, who said 'Lo, I come... mine ears hast thou opened (digged),' Psalm 40:6-8, applied to Christ, Hebrews 10:5-7, 'a body hast thou prepared me,' Christ the Servant who, out of love for his Father and his bride, the church, and his children, would not go free but gave himself to perpetual service and obedience even unto death, and the believer's glad, willing, perpetual bondservice to Christ, the ear bored to hear and obey, Romans 6:18-22, 'servants to righteousness,' the love-service of the redeemed, 2 Corinthians 5:14); 'he shall serve him for ever' (the perpetual service, for ever, that is, to the jubilee, Leviticus 25:40, or for life, the willing, lifelong bondage, the providence, the type of the everlasting, willing service of love, the bondservant of Christ for ever) — Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the servant's bored ear and perpetual service signify?

sides.position: The willing bondservant bound by love - the ear (the organ of obedience) bored, marking perpetual, willing service; a type of Christ the willing Servant who would not go free but gave himself to perpetual obedience (Psalm 40:6-8; Hebrews 10:5-7), and of the believer's glad bondservice to Christ (Romans 6:18-22)

themes: Then his master shall bring him unto the judges... and his master shall bore his ear through with an aul; and he shall serve him for ever
the servant's bored ear and perpetual service (the crux of the willing bondservant)
the love-bound servant bound to hear and obey for ever
a type of Christ the willing Servant (Psalm 40:6-8; Hebrews 10:5-7) and the believer's bondservice (Romans 6:18-22)

schools.summary: The law of the maidservant sold by her father: 'and if a man sell his daughter to be a maidservant, she shall not go out as the menservants do' — a daughter sold (by a poor father, into servitude, usually with a view to marriage, vv.8-11) not to go out free in the seventh year as the menservants did (the special case, the maidservant, sold for marriage, under different protections, the law's care, the maidservant safeguarded, not cast off, vv.8-11); the daughter sold (by poverty, into service, with a view to marriage) — the case of the bondmaid (the provision for her protection); 'she shall not go out as the menservants do' (not freed at the seventh year as the men, her case different, for she was taken with a view to marriage, vv.8-11, the law's protection, the maidservant not to be wronged, but married or redeemed, vv.8-11, the providence, the care of the law for the vulnerable) — Calvin, Gill, Ellicott.

themes: And if a man sell his daughter to be a maidservant, she shall not go out as the menservants do
the law of the maidservant sold by her father
the special case of the maidservant taken with a view to marriage
under the law's protection, not to be wronged (vv.8-11)

schools.summary: The protection of the betrothed maidservant: 'if she please not her master, who hath betrothed her to himself, then shall he let her be redeemed: to sell her unto a strange nation he shall have no power, seeing he hath dealt deceitfully with her' — if the master, having betrothed the maidservant to himself, was not pleased with her, he must let her be redeemed (bought back by her family, the protection, the maidservant not to be cast off without recourse, but redeemed, the law's care, the betrothed maid safeguarded); the master had no power to sell her to a foreign people (a strange nation) — the protection (the maid not to be trafficked abroad, the limit on the master's power); 'seeing he hath dealt deceitfully with her' (the master's dealing deceitfully, breaking faith, not keeping the betrothal, the ground of the redemption, the law's justice, the wronged maid protected, the providence, the care of the law for the betrayed and vulnerable) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley, JFB.

themes: If she please not her master, who hath betrothed her to himself, then shall he let her be redeemed... seeing he hath dealt deceitfully with her
the protection of the betrothed maidservant
the wronged maid safeguarded, to be redeemed not cast off
not to be trafficked abroad, the law's care for the vulnerable

schools.summary: The maidservant betrothed to the son treated as a daughter: 'and if he have betrothed her unto his son, he shall deal with her after the manner of daughters' — if the master betrothed the maidservant to his son, he must treat her as a daughter (with the full rights and dignity of a daughter-in-law, not a slave, the elevation, the bondmaid raised to the status of a daughter, the law's dignity, the maidservant honored, not degraded); the maidservant given to the son in marriage — the elevation (the honor); 'he shall deal with her after the manner of daughters' (the maidservant treated as a daughter, with full rights, dignity, provision, the elevation of the bondmaid, the law's care for her honor, the providence, the protection and dignity of the vulnerable, a faint figure, some note, of the grace that raises the bondservant to a daughter, Galatians 4:7, 'no more a servant, but a son') — Wesley, Gill.

themes: And if he have betrothed her unto his son, he shall deal with her after the manner of daughters
the maidservant betrothed to the son treated as a daughter
the bondmaid raised to the status and rights of a daughter
a faint figure of grace raising the servant to a daughter (Galatians 4:7)

schools.summary: The maidservant-wife's rights protected: 'if he take him another wife; her food, her raiment, and her duty of marriage, shall he not diminish' — if the master (or son) who married the maidservant took another wife, he must not diminish the first wife's food, raiment, and marriage rights (conjugal duty, the protection of the wife, her threefold rights secured, the law's justice, the maidservant-wife not to be wronged or neglected for another); the food and raiment (the wife's maintenance, not to be diminished) — the provision secured (the wife's rights); 'her duty of marriage, shall he not diminish' (the conjugal right, not to be withheld, the threefold protection, food, raiment, marriage rights, the basic dues of a wife, 1 Corinthians 7:3-5, the law's care, the wife protected against neglect, the providence, the dignity of the maidservant-wife secured) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: If he take him another wife; her food, her raiment, and her duty of marriage, shall he not diminish
the maidservant-wife's rights protected
the threefold dues of a wife secured - food, raiment, marriage rights (1 Corinthians 7:3-5)
the wife not to be wronged or neglected for another

schools.summary: The maidservant's freedom if her rights are denied: 'and if he do not these three unto her, then shall she go out free without money' — if the master failed in the three duties (food, raiment, marriage rights, v.10), the maidservant to go out free, without payment (the release, the wronged maid freed, the law's justice, the failure of duty forfeiting the master's claim, the protection, the maid's freedom secured if neglected); the three duties (v.10) failed — the ground of the release (the master's forfeiture); 'then shall she go out free without money' (the maidservant freed, without paying for her freedom, if her rights were denied, the law's mercy and justice, the wronged maid released, the providence, the protection of the vulnerable, the master's neglect punished by the loss of his servant) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And if he do not these three unto her, then shall she go out free without money
the maidservant's freedom if her rights are denied
the wronged maid freed without payment (v.10)
the master's neglect punished by the loss of his claim

schools.summary: The law of murder and the sanctity of life: 'he that smiteth a man, so that he die, shall be surely put to death' — whoever struck a man so that he died to be surely put to death (the law of murder, the deliberate killing of a man punished by death, the sanctity of human life, the image of God in man, Genesis 9:6, 'whoso sheddeth man's blood, by man shall his blood be shed: for in the image of God made he man,' the capital law, the shedding of innocent blood requiring the life of the murderer); the smiting unto death (the killing of a man) — the crime of murder (the violation of the sixth commandment, Exodus 20:13); 'shall be surely put to death' (the death penalty for murder, the surety of the punishment, no ransom for the murderer, Numbers 35:31, the sanctity of life, the murderer's life forfeit, Genesis 9:6, the providence, the magistrate's sword against the shedder of blood, Romans 13:4, the public justice) — Henry, Gill, Calvin, Keil.

themes: He that smiteth a man, so that he die, shall be surely put to death
the law of murder and the sanctity of life
the sanctity of human life, the image of God in man (Genesis 9:6)
the murderer's life forfeit, the public justice (Numbers 35:31; Romans 13:4)

schools.summary: The place of refuge for the unintentional manslayer: 'and if a man lie not in wait, but God deliver him into his hand; then I will appoint thee a place whither he shall flee' — if a man killed another not by lying in wait (not of deliberate malice), but 'God delivered him into his hand' (by accident, by God's providence overruling), God appointing a place (a city of refuge) to which he might flee (the distinction, murder, of malice aforethought, from manslaughter, by accident, the providence, even the accident overruled by God, 'God deliver him into his hand'); 'God deliver him into his hand' — the accidental killing ascribed, in a sense, to God's providence (the mysterious overruling, even of the unintended death, Deuteronomy 19:4-5, not that God is the author of sin, but that the event, unintended by the slayer, falls under God's sovereign ordering, the providence in all events, even the accident, God's hand, the lesson, the distinction of intent, malice from misfortune); 'then I will appoint thee a place whither he shall flee' (the place of refuge, the cities of refuge, Numbers 35:6-15; Deuteronomy 19:1-13; Joshua 20, appointed for the manslayer, the mercy, the unintentional slayer protected from the avenger of blood, the type, the cities of refuge a type of Christ the refuge, the sinner fleeing to Christ from the avenging law, Hebrews 6:18, 'fled for refuge to lay hold upon the hope set before us') — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

debates.question: What does the place of refuge and 'God deliver him into his hand' teach?

sides.position: The distinction of murder (of malice) from manslaughter (by accident, the providence overruling even the unintended death, Deuteronomy 19:4-5), and the place of refuge for the manslayer (the cities of refuge, Numbers 35:6-15; Joshua 20) - a type of Christ the refuge to whom the sinner flees from the avenging law (Hebrews 6:18)

themes: And if a man lie not in wait, but God deliver him into his hand; then I will appoint thee a place whither he shall flee
the place of refuge for the unintentional manslayer (the crux of the distinction)
the distinction of murder (malice) from manslaughter (accident, God's providence overruling, Deuteronomy 19:4-5)
the cities of refuge (Numbers 35:6-15; Joshua 20), a type of Christ the refuge (Hebrews 6:18)

schools.summary: The presumptuous murderer taken even from the altar: 'but if a man come presumptuously upon his neighbour, to slay him with guile; thou shalt take him from mine altar, that he may die' — the presumptuous murderer (who killed with malice and guile, deliberately) to be taken even from God's altar to be put to death (the deliberate murder, of malice aforethought, distinguished from the accidental, v.13, the no refuge for the murderer, not even the altar could shelter the deliberate killer); the slaying with guile (deliberate, treacherous murder) — the malice aforethought (the crime that found no refuge); 'thou shalt take him from mine altar, that he may die' (the murderer taken even from the altar, the altar no sanctuary for the deliberate killer, 1 Kings 2:28-34, Joab dragged from the altar, the justice, no refuge for the murderer, the lesson, the altar, the place of mercy, no shelter for the presumptuous, the contrast with the city of refuge for the innocent, v.13, no sacrifice or sanctuary avails for the willful sinner, Hebrews 10:26-27) — Calvin, Gill, Ellicott.

themes: But if a man come presumptuously upon his neighbour, to slay him with guile; thou shalt take him from mine altar, that he may die
the presumptuous murderer taken even from the altar
no refuge for the deliberate killer, even at the altar (1 Kings 2:28-34)
no sanctuary avails for the willful sinner (Hebrews 10:26-27)

schools.summary: The death penalty for smiting a parent: 'and he that smiteth his father, or his mother, shall be surely put to death' — whoever struck (not necessarily killed, but violently assaulted) his father or mother to be put to death (the severity, the crime against parents capital, even short of murder, the honor of parents, the fifth commandment, Exodus 20:12, enforced with the utmost severity, the sanctity of the parental bond); the smiting of a parent (the violent assault on father or mother) — the heinous crime (the violation of the honor due to parents, Exodus 20:12); 'shall be surely put to death' (the death penalty, the severity marking the heinousness of the crime against parents, the honor of parents enforced, the fifth commandment, the foundation of family and society, the providence, the law guarding the parental bond, the order of the family) — Keil and Delitzsch, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: And he that smiteth his father, or his mother, shall be surely put to death
the death penalty for smiting a parent
the crime against parents capital, even short of murder
the honor of parents enforced (the fifth commandment, Exodus 20:12)

schools.summary: The death penalty for manstealing: 'and he that stealeth a man, and selleth him, or if he be found in his hand, he shall surely be put to death' — whoever stole a man (kidnapped him) and sold him, or held him, to be put to death (the law against manstealing, kidnapping, the slave-trade, the severity, the crime capital, the man's liberty and person sacred, the protection of human freedom, the stealing of a man a capital offense, Deuteronomy 24:7); the manstealing (kidnapping and selling) — the heinous crime (the violation of the man's person and liberty, 1 Timothy 1:10, 'menstealers' among the lawless); 'he shall surely be put to death' (the death penalty for manstealing, the severity marking the sanctity of human liberty, the condemnation of the slave-trade, the stealing and selling of men, Deuteronomy 24:7; 1 Timothy 1:10, the providence, the law guarding the freedom and person of man, made in God's image) — Clarke, Gill.

themes: And he that stealeth a man, and selleth him, or if he be found in his hand, he shall surely be put to death
the death penalty for manstealing
the sanctity of human liberty, kidnapping and selling men capital (Deuteronomy 24:7)
the slave-trade condemned (1 Timothy 1:10)

schools.summary: The death penalty for cursing a parent: 'and he that curseth his father, or his mother, shall surely be put to death' — whoever cursed (reviled, spoke evil of, dishonored with the tongue) his father or mother to be put to death (the severity, the crime against parents, even in word, capital, the honor of parents, the fifth commandment, Exodus 20:12, enforced even against the cursing tongue, the sanctity of the parental bond, the Lord himself citing this, Matthew 15:4; Mark 7:10, 'He that curseth father or mother, let him die the death'); the cursing of a parent (reviling, dishonoring with the tongue) — the heinous crime (the violation of the honor due, Leviticus 20:9); 'shall surely be put to death' (the death penalty, the crime against parents capital even in word, the honor of parents enforced, the fifth commandment, cited by Christ, Matthew 15:4, the providence, the law guarding the parental bond, the foundation of family and society) — Gill.

themes: And he that curseth his father, or his mother, shall surely be put to death
the death penalty for cursing a parent
the crime against parents capital even in word
the honor of parents enforced, cited by Christ (Exodus 20:12; Matthew 15:4)

schools.summary: The law of injury in a quarrel: 'and if men strive together, and one smite another with a stone, or with his fist, and he die not, but keepeth his bed' — if men quarreled and one struck the other (with a stone or fist) so that he did not die but was laid up (kept his bed, injured, the case of injury short of death, the assault in a quarrel, the wound not fatal, the law of compensation, vv.19, the smiter liable for the loss and the healing); the smiting in a quarrel (with stone or fist, the wound) — the assault (the injury); 'he die not, but keepeth his bed' (the injured man laid up, not killed, but disabled, the case, the assault short of death, the providence, the law of compensation, vv.19, the smiter liable, the justice between man and man) — Keil and Delitzsch, Calvin, Gill.

themes: And if men strive together, and one smite another with a stone, or with his fist, and he die not, but keepeth his bed
the law of injury in a quarrel
the assault short of death, the injured man laid up
the law of compensation following (vv.19)

schools.summary: The compensation for the injured man's loss: 'if he rise again, and walk abroad upon his staff, then shall he that smote him be quit: only he shall pay for the loss of his time, and shall cause him to be thoroughly healed' — if the injured man recovered (rose and walked abroad), the smiter acquitted of the capital charge, but must pay for the injured man's lost time and his healing (the medical costs, the compensation, the smiter liable for the loss and the cure, the justice, the wrong made good, the injured man not left to bear the loss); the smiter acquitted (of the capital charge, since the man lived) — the justice (no death penalty, the man recovered, the liability remaining, for the loss); 'he shall pay for the loss of his time, and shall cause him to be thoroughly healed' (the smiter paying for the lost time, the wages lost, and the healing, the medical care, the restitution, the wrong made good, the providence, the equity of the law, the injured man compensated) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: If he rise again, and walk abroad upon his staff, then shall he that smote him be quit: only he shall pay for the loss of his time, and shall cause him to be thoroughly healed
the compensation for the injured man's loss
the smiter liable for the lost time and the healing
the wrong made good, the equity of the law

schools.summary: The law protecting the servant from a fatal beating: 'and if a man smite his servant, or his maid, with a rod, and he die under his hand; he shall be surely punished' — if a master beat his servant (or maid) with a rod so that the servant died under his hand (immediately), the master to be punished (the protection of the servant, even the bondservant's life guarded by the law, the limit on the master's power, the servant not a mere chattel, but a person whose life was protected); the servant dying under the master's hand (immediately, from the beating) — the fatal blow (the master's guilt); 'he shall be surely punished' (the master punished, avenged, likely with death, as a murderer, vv.12, the exact penalty debated, but the master held accountable for the servant's death, the protection of the servant, the law guarding even the slave's life, a remarkable mercy in the ancient world, where slaves were chattels, the providence, the dignity of the servant as a person, the master accountable) — Calvin, Gill, JFB, Wesley.

themes: And if a man smite his servant, or his maid, with a rod, and he die under his hand; he shall be surely punished
the law protecting the servant from a fatal beating
even the bondservant's life guarded by the law, the master accountable
the dignity of the servant as a person, a mercy in the ancient world

schools.summary: The master not punished if the servant survives: 'notwithstanding, if he continue a day or two, he shall not be punished: for he is his money' — if the smitten servant survived a day or two (and then perhaps recovered, or died later, not immediately), the master not punished (with death), since the death was presumed not deliberate, and the master suffered loss in the servant himself ('he is his money,' the master's own property, his loss being his punishment, the case, the servant surviving, the death not clearly deliberate, the master's loss, the servant his property, the loss a check on cruelty); the servant surviving (a day or two) — the death not immediate (the case presumed accidental or not deliberate, the master not capitally punished); 'for he is his money' (the master's loss in the servant, his property, his money, a check on cruelty, the master not lightly destroying his own, the providence, the law presuming the master would not deliberately destroy his own property, yet not licensing cruelty, the servant's life still protected, v.20) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: Notwithstanding, if he continue a day or two, he shall not be punished: for he is his money
the master not punished if the servant survives
the death not deliberate, the case presumed not malicious
the master's loss in his servant a check on cruelty (v.20)

schools.summary: The law of harm to a pregnant woman: 'if men strive, and hurt a woman with child, so that her fruit depart from her, and yet no mischief follow: he shall be surely punished, according as the woman's husband will lay upon him; and he shall pay as the judges determine' — if men quarreling injured a pregnant woman so that she miscarried (her fruit departed) but 'no mischief' (no further harm, no death of the woman) followed, the offender to be fined (punished as the husband and the judges determined, the case, the harm to the pregnant woman, the premature birth or miscarriage, the value set on the unborn, the injury punished, the life in the womb protected); the reading — 'her fruit depart' (a premature birth, or miscarriage); 'no mischief' (no death, of mother or, on one reading, of the child) — the witnesses debating whether 'mischief' refers to harm to the mother only, or to mother or child (the value of the unborn, the injury to the child in the womb punished, the life in the womb under the law's protection, a witness to the sanctity of unborn life); 'he shall be surely punished, according as the woman's husband will lay upon him; and he shall pay as the judges determine' (the fine, set by the husband, ratified by the judges, the compensation, the harm made good, the providence, the law protecting the pregnant woman and her unborn child, the value of the life in the womb) — Henry, Gill, Calvin.

debates.question: Does 'no mischief follow' refer to the mother only, or the unborn child also?

sides.position: The witnesses debate whether 'mischief' means harm to the mother only, or to mother or child; either way the injury to the pregnant woman and the life in the womb is punished, a witness to the value and protection of unborn life

themes: If men strive, and hurt a woman with child, so that her fruit depart from her, and yet no mischief follow: he shall be surely punished... as the judges determine
the law of harm to a pregnant woman
the injury to mother and unborn child punished
the life in the womb under the law's protection, a witness to the value of unborn life

schools.summary: The law of life for life: 'and if any mischief follow, then thou shalt give life for life' — if 'mischief' (serious harm, the death of the woman, or on one reading the child) followed the injury (v.22), then 'life for life' required (the law of proportion, the punishment matching the harm, life for a life lost, the sanctity of life, the death of the woman, or child, requiring life, the principle of the lex talionis, vv.24-25, the just measure of retribution); the 'mischief' (the death, or grievous harm) following the injury — the serious case (the harm requiring proportionate penalty); 'thou shalt give life for life' (the life for life, the just retribution, the death of the offender for the life lost, the principle, the lex talionis, the punishment proportioned to the crime, vv.24-25; Genesis 9:6; Leviticus 24:17-21, the providence, the equity of the law, the sanctity of life, the just measure of justice) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And if any mischief follow, then thou shalt give life for life
the law of life for life
the punishment proportioned to the harm (the lex talionis)
the sanctity of life, the just retribution (Genesis 9:6; Leviticus 24:17)

schools.summary: The lex talionis - eye for eye: 'eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot' — the great rule — 'Eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot': the law of retaliation (the lex talionis), the punishment exactly proportioned to the injury (eye for eye, etc., Leviticus 24:19-20; Deuteronomy 19:21, the witnesses expounding the purpose — not the licensing of private revenge, but the rule for the magistrate, the just measure of public justice, the penalty matching the crime, the restraint of revenge, the law setting a limit, not more than eye for eye, curbing the excess of vengeance, the equity, the exact proportion of justice, the lesson — most witnesses noting this was usually carried out by a fixed compensation, a money payment for the injury, the eye, etc., reckoned in value, not literal mutilation, except for life, v.23, and the witnesses noting Christ's word — the Lord, in the Sermon on the Mount, setting aside the misuse of this rule as a warrant for private revenge, 'ye have heard that it hath been said, An eye for an eye... but I say unto you, That ye resist not evil,' Matthew 5:38-39, not abolishing the magistrate's justice, but forbidding the private spirit of retaliation, calling his people to forbearance and love of enemies); the exact proportion (tooth, hand, foot, etc.) — the just measure (the penalty matching the harm, the restraint, the limit set on retribution, no more than the injury); the lex talionis — the rule of public justice (the magistrate's measure, not private revenge, the providence, the equity of the law, the restraint of vengeance, fulfilled and transcended in Christ's call to forbearance, Matthew 5:38-39; Romans 12:17-21) — Calvin, Wesley, Gill, Clarke.

debates.question: What is the purpose of 'eye for eye, tooth for tooth'?

sides.position: The rule of public justice (the magistrate's measure), the penalty exactly proportioned to the crime (Leviticus 24:19-20; Deuteronomy 19:21) - not licensing private revenge but restraining it (no more than the injury), usually by fixed compensation; reclaimed by Christ from its misuse as a warrant for private retaliation, calling his people to forbearance (Matthew 5:38-39; Romans 12:17-21)

themes: Eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot
the lex talionis - eye for eye (the crux of just retribution)
the rule of public justice, the penalty proportioned to the crime, restraining revenge (Leviticus 24:19-20; Deuteronomy 19:21)
reclaimed by Christ from its misuse for private retaliation, calling to forbearance (Matthew 5:38-39; Romans 12:17-21)

schools.summary: The lex talionis continued: 'burning for burning, wound for wound, stripe for stripe' — the law of retaliation continued (burning for burning, wound for wound, stripe for stripe, the exact proportion, the penalty matching the injury, in every kind of hurt, the just measure, the rule of public justice, vv.24, the completeness, the principle applied to all injuries, the punishment fitting the crime); the exact correspondence (burning, wound, stripe) — the just measure (the penalty matching the harm); 'stripe for stripe' (the principle applied to every injury, the just proportion in all, the rule of public justice, the magistrate's measure, not private revenge, vv.24, the providence, the equity of the law, the restraint of vengeance, fulfilled in Christ's call to forbearance, Matthew 5:38-39, and the wounds of Christ, by whose stripes we are healed, Isaiah 53:5; 1 Peter 2:24, bearing the penalty we deserved) — Gill.

themes: Burning for burning, wound for wound, stripe for stripe
the lex talionis continued
the penalty matching the harm in every kind of injury (vv.24)
the wounds Christ bore in our stead (Isaiah 53:5; 1 Peter 2:24)

schools.summary: The maimed servant freed for the loss of an eye: 'and if a man smite the eye of his servant, or the eye of his maid, that it perish; he shall let him go free for his eye's sake' — if a master struck out the eye of his servant (or maid), he must let the servant go free for the eye's sake (the protection of the servant, the master's cruelty punished by the loss of the servant, the mercy of the law, even the bondservant's body protected, the master's power limited, the freedom, the maimed servant freed in compensation for the injury); the destroying of the servant's eye (the master's cruelty) — the injury (the wrong to the servant); 'he shall let him go free for his eye's sake' (the servant freed, in compensation for the lost eye, the law's mercy, the servant's freedom the penalty of the master's cruelty, the providence, the protection of the servant, the master deterred from cruelty by the loss of his servant, the dignity of the servant's body) — Calvin, Gill, JFB.

themes: And if a man smite the eye of his servant, or the eye of his maid, that it perish; he shall let him go free for his eye's sake
the maimed servant freed for the loss of an eye
the master's cruelty punished by the loss of the servant
even the bondservant's body protected, the dignity of the servant

schools.summary: The servant freed even for the loss of a tooth: 'and if he smite out his manservant's tooth, or his maidservant's tooth; he shall let him go free for his tooth's sake' — if a master knocked out even a tooth of his servant (or maid), he must let the servant go free for the tooth's sake (the protection of the servant, even a small injury, a tooth, freeing the servant, the mercy of the law, the master's cruelty, even in a small thing, punished by the loss of the servant, the freedom, the servant freed for even the least permanent injury); the knocking out of a tooth (even a small injury) — the master's cruelty (the wrong, even in a small thing); 'he shall let him go free for his tooth's sake' (the servant freed, even for a tooth, the law's mercy, even the least permanent injury freeing the servant, the lesson, the dignity of the servant's body, the master deterred from all cruelty, the providence, the protection of the servant, the value of even the lowest member) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And if he smite out his manservant's tooth, or his maidservant's tooth; he shall let him go free for his tooth's sake
the servant freed even for the loss of a tooth
even the least injury freeing the servant
the dignity of the servant's body, the master deterred from all cruelty

schools.summary: The law of the goring ox and the value of human life: 'if an ox gore a man or a woman, that they die: then the ox shall be surely stoned, and his flesh shall not be eaten; but the owner of the ox shall be quit' — if an ox (not known to be dangerous) gored a person to death, the ox to be stoned (its flesh not eaten), but the owner (not at fault) acquitted (the case, the goring ox, the owner not previously warned, the stoning of the ox, the beast that took human life destroyed, the value of human blood, Genesis 9:5, 'at the hand of every beast will I require it'); the ox stoned (destroyed for taking human life), its flesh not eaten (as a thing accursed, defiled by the blood) — the sanctity of human life (even the beast that kills a man destroyed, Genesis 9:5); 'but the owner of the ox shall be quit' (the owner acquitted, not at fault, the ox not known to be dangerous, the justice, no liability where no negligence, the providence, the equity of the law, the value of human life upheld, the owner cleared where guiltless) — Calvin, Gill, Ellicott.

themes: If an ox gore a man or a woman, that they die: then the ox shall be surely stoned, and his flesh shall not be eaten; but the owner of the ox shall be quit
the law of the goring ox and the value of human life
the beast that kills a man destroyed, the value of human blood (Genesis 9:5)
the owner cleared where guiltless, no liability without negligence

schools.summary: The negligent owner of a dangerous ox held guilty: 'but if the ox were wont to push with his horn in time past, and it hath been testified to his owner, and he hath not kept him in, but that he hath killed a man or a woman; the ox shall be stoned, and his owner also shall be put to death' — if the ox was known to be dangerous (had gored before, the owner warned) and the owner failed to keep him in, and the ox killed someone, both the ox and the negligent owner liable (the owner put to death, unless ransomed, v.30, the negligence, the owner culpable for not restraining the known danger, the liability, the owner's neglect treated as virtual manslaughter); the owner warned (the ox known to gore) yet not restraining him — the culpable negligence (the owner's fault); 'the ox shall be stoned, and his owner also shall be put to death' (the owner liable to death, for his negligence, treated as manslaughter, unless ransomed, v.30, the justice, the negligent owner accountable for the death his carelessness caused, the providence, the law's care for human life, negligence punished, the duty to restrain known dangers) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: But if the ox were wont to push with his horn in time past, and it hath been testified to his owner, and he hath not kept him in... the ox shall be stoned, and his owner also shall be put to death
the negligent owner of a dangerous ox held guilty
the owner's negligence treated as virtual manslaughter
the law's care for human life, the duty to restrain known dangers

schools.summary: The ransom permitted for the negligent owner: 'if there be laid on him a sum of money, then he shall give for the ransom of his life whatsoever is laid upon him' — if a ransom (a sum of money) was set, the negligent owner (v.29) might give it for the ransom of his life (the death penalty commuted to a fine, since his guilt was negligence, not deliberate murder, the ransom, the mitigation, the life redeemed by a payment, the distinction, negligence ransomable, deliberate murder not, Numbers 35:31, 'ye shall take no satisfaction for the life of a murderer'); the ransom (the price of the owner's life, the commutation of the penalty) — the mitigation (the life redeemed by payment); 'whatsoever is laid upon him' (the sum set, by the judges, or the family of the dead, the ransom, the payment for the life, the providence, the distinction of negligence from murder, the mercy in the ransom, yet the seriousness of the negligence marked, a faint figure, some note, of the ransom for life, the one true ransom in Christ, 1 Timothy 2:6; Matthew 20:28, who gave his life a ransom) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: If there be laid on him a sum of money, then he shall give for the ransom of his life whatsoever is laid upon him
the ransom permitted for the negligent owner
negligence ransomable, deliberate murder not (Numbers 35:31)
a faint figure of the one true ransom in Christ (1 Timothy 2:6; Matthew 20:28)

schools.summary: The same law for a son or daughter gored: 'whether he have gored a son, or have gored a daughter, according to this judgment shall it be done unto him' — the same law (vv.28-30) applying whether the ox gored a son or a daughter (a free child, not only an adult, the equity, the same justice for all, son or daughter, the value of every life, the child's life as protected as the adult's, the daughter's as the son's); the same law for son or daughter (no distinction of sex or age) — the equity (the equal value of every life); 'according to this judgment shall it be done unto him' (the same rule applied, vv.28-30, the impartiality, the same justice for all, the providence, the equity of the law, the value of every human life, son or daughter alike) — Calvin, Gill.

themes: Whether he have gored a son, or have gored a daughter, according to this judgment shall it be done unto him
the same law for a son or daughter gored
the equal value of every life, son or daughter alike
the impartial justice of the law

schools.summary: The thirty shekels for a gored servant: 'if the ox shall push a manservant or a maidservant; he shall give unto their master thirty shekels of silver, and the ox shall be stoned' — the sum — 'he shall give unto their master thirty shekels of silver': if the ox gored a bondservant (man or maid), the owner of the ox paying the servant's master thirty shekels of silver (the set price, the valuation of a slave), and the ox stoned (the price of a slave, thirty shekels the standard value of a bondservant, the witnesses noting the striking application — thirty shekels of silver was the price afterward set on Christ, the betrayal-money: 'they weighed for my price thirty pieces of silver,' Zechariah 11:12-13; 'they covenanted with him for thirty pieces of silver,' Matthew 26:15; 27:3-10, the Lord of glory valued at the price of a gored slave, the shame and the irony — the Son of God priced as a bondservant, sold for the value of a slave killed by an ox, the depth of his humiliation, taking the form of a servant, Philippians 2:7, and valued as one); the ox stoned (as in the case of the free person, vv.28) — the beast destroyed (the value of life, even the servant's life avenged on the beast); the thirty shekels — the price of a slave (the valuation of a bondservant), the very sum for which Christ was betrayed (Zechariah 11:12-13; Matthew 26:15, the providence, the ancient price-of-a-slave pointing, in God's ordering, to the betrayal-price of the Servant of the Lord) — Calvin, Gill.

debates.question: What is the significance of the thirty shekels for a gored servant?

sides.position: The standard valuation of a bondservant (the price of a slave), and in God's striking providence the very price afterward set on Christ, the betrayal-money of Judas (Zechariah 11:12-13; Matthew 26:15; 27:3-10) - the Lord of glory valued at the price of a gored slave, the depth of his humiliation as the Servant of the Lord (Philippians 2:7)

themes: If the ox shall push a manservant or a maidservant; he shall give unto their master thirty shekels of silver, and the ox shall be stoned
the thirty shekels for a gored servant (the crux of the price of a slave)
the standard valuation of a bondservant, thirty shekels of silver
the very price set on Christ, the betrayal-money (Zechariah 11:12-13; Matthew 26:15; 27:3-10), the depth of his humiliation (Philippians 2:7)

schools.summary: The law of the open pit and negligence: 'and if a man shall open a pit, or if a man shall dig a pit, and not cover it, and an ox or an ass fall therein' — if a man opened or dug a pit (a cistern, well) and left it uncovered, and an animal (ox or ass) fell in and died, the digger liable (the negligence, the uncovered pit a danger, the owner responsible for the harm it caused, the case, the law of liability for negligence, the duty to guard against foreseeable harm to a neighbour's property); the uncovered pit (the negligence, the failure to guard the danger) — the carelessness (the liability); 'an ox or an ass fall therein' (the animal falling and dying, the harm from the negligence, the liability, the digger to make restitution, v.34, the providence, the law's care for the neighbour's property, negligence punished, the duty to prevent foreseeable harm) — Calvin, Gill.

themes: And if a man shall open a pit, or if a man shall dig a pit, and not cover it, and an ox or an ass fall therein
the law of the open pit and negligence
the uncovered pit a danger, the owner liable for the harm
the duty to guard against foreseeable harm to a neighbour's property

schools.summary: The restitution for the animal killed in the pit: 'the owner of the pit shall make it good, and give money unto the owner of them; and the dead beast shall be his' — the owner of the pit to make good the loss (pay the value of the animal to its owner), and keep the dead beast (worth something for its hide or carcass, the restitution, the negligent party paying for the harm, receiving the carcass, the equity, the wrong made good, the loss compensated, the just settlement); the restitution (the value paid to the animal's owner) — the wrong made good (the loss compensated); 'the dead beast shall be his' (the dead animal, its carcass, worth something, given to the one who paid for it, the negligent party, who bore the loss but kept the carcass, the equity, the fair settlement, the loss and the residue justly apportioned, the providence, the equity of the law, the wrong made good) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill.

themes: The owner of the pit shall make it good, and give money unto the owner of them; and the dead beast shall be his
the restitution for the animal killed in the pit
the wrong made good, the loss compensated
the fair settlement, the equity of the law

schools.summary: The equitable division when one ox kills another: 'and if one man's ox hurt another's, that he die; then they shall sell the live ox, and divide the money of it; and the dead ox also they shall divide' — if one man's ox killed another's (where neither owner was at fault, the ox not known to be dangerous), they selling the live ox and dividing the money, and dividing the dead ox too (the equitable division, the loss shared, where neither was to blame, the fairness, the loss borne equally, the just apportioning); the live ox sold and the money divided (the loss shared) — the equity (the fair division); 'the dead ox also they shall divide' (the dead ox divided too, the carcass shared, the equity, the loss borne equally where neither was at fault, the providence, the fairness of the law, the loss justly apportioned) — Gill.

themes: And if one man's ox hurt another's, that he die; then they shall sell the live ox, and divide the money of it; and the dead ox also they shall divide
the equitable division when one ox kills another
the loss shared where neither owner was at fault
the just apportioning, the fairness of the law

schools.summary: The full liability of the negligent owner: 'or if it be known that the ox hath used to push in time past, and his owner hath not kept him in; he shall surely pay ox for ox; and the dead shall be his own' — if the ox was known to be dangerous (had gored before, the owner warned) and the owner failed to restrain him, the owner fully liable (paying ox for ox, a whole live ox for the dead one), keeping the dead carcass (the full liability, the negligent owner bearing the whole loss, not sharing it as in v.35, the negligence, the owner's fault, the known danger unrestrained); the negligent owner paying ox for ox (the full value, since he was warned and culpable) — the full liability (the negligence punished); 'the dead shall be his own' (the dead ox kept by the negligent owner, who paid the full value and bore the loss, the justice, the negligence fully charged, the providence, the equity of the law, negligence punished, the contrast with the no-fault case, v.35, the duty to restrain known dangers) — Gill.

themes: Or if it be known that the ox hath used to push in time past, and his owner hath not kept him in; he shall surely pay ox for ox; and the dead shall be his own
the full liability of the negligent owner
the negligent owner bearing the whole loss, not sharing it (contrast v.35)
the duty to restrain known dangers, negligence fully charged
